fact: 295D:0504 is Reactor 50's DFU-mode PID.
295D:0502 is Reactor 100's DFU-mode PID.
295D:0222 is Spark LIVE's DFU-mode PID.
295D:0232 is Spark 2's DFU-mode PID.
295D:0506 is a CDC PID reported by Reactor 50 and Reactor 100 in normal app mode.
295D:0507 is a CDC PID reported by Reactor 50 and Reactor 100 in normal app mode.
dfu-util.exe lives at D:\mybot\git\tool\dfu-util-0.11-binaries\win64\dfu-util.exe.
esptool.exe lives at D:\mybot\git\tool\esptool-v4.5.1-win64\esptool.exe.
Jenkins base URL is https://jk-builds.positivegrid.com/jenkins/; the older jenkins.positivegrid.com is deprecated and its DNS no longer resolves.
Jenkins API token for user alice.chan@positivegrid.com is stored at D:\mybot\git\tool\.jenkins-token.
REACTOR_AMP_FW is the Jenkins job that builds Reactor MCU firmware; it is the CopyArtifact source for MCU_BUILD, copying **/*.bin to Images/.
REACTOR_ESP32_FW is the Jenkins job that builds Reactor ESP32 firmware; it is the CopyArtifact source for ESP_BUILD, copying **/*.bin to Images/ESP32bin/.

MCU_BUILD and ESP_BUILD are Jenkins BuildSelectorParameter fields; the API trigger must send XML like <SpecificBuildSelector><buildNumber>148</buildNumber></SpecificBuildSelector> — a plain number causes a 500 ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

External NOR chip-erase during DFU takes 10-50 seconds; stock dfu-util 0.11 may time out before it completes. Ken Hung built a longer-timeout version, at GitLab desktop/SparkLiveFwUpgradeLauncher/tree/master/Import/dfu-util.
The get_status error printed by dfu-util after :leave is an expected warning and does not indicate a failed flash.
The dfu-util "Invalid DFU suffix signature" warning is expected/normal and does not affect functionality.
Device serial number is stored in MCU internal flash at 0x0800FFE0, 32 bytes, ASCII string null-padded.
These products share one USB architecture: a single PC bus-powered USB hub with 3 endpoints beneath it — MCU (STM32), ESP32, and BT Audio chip.
The BT Audio chip is independently PC(USB bus)-powered and not controlled by the MCU; it stays enumerated through MCU reboot/DFU, making it a reliable persistent detection anchor.
ESP32 power/EN is controlled by the MCU; ESP32 only enumerates after MCU boot pulls EN high, and disappears when the MCU loses power.
295D:0503 is Reactor 50's BT Audio chip (an Actions chip, not the MCU), and serves as its USB audio anchor device.
295D:0501 is Reactor 100's BT Audio chip (an Actions chip, not the MCU), and serves as its USB audio anchor device.
Spark 2's audio anchor device is 295D:0231.
Spark LIVE's audio anchor device is 0D8C:0033, a third-party C-MEDIA chip, not a Positive Grid VID.
The previously assumed Spark LIVE audio anchor 295D:0221 (extrapolated from Spark 2's 0231, no spec source, git commit ffdd3dc) has been confirmed incorrect; the real anchor is 0D8C:0033.
Spark EDGE's audio anchor is assumed to be 295D:0251, extrapolated from Spark 2's pattern; not yet verified on real hardware, and the analogous LIVE guess (0221) turned out wrong.
0D8C:0033 is a generic C-MEDIA USB audio chip ID also reported by common third-party USB soundcards/headsets, not exclusive to Spark LIVE hardware.
When the MCU enters DFU mode, the BT Audio device does not disappear (it is independently powered); the DFU device appears as a sibling on the same hub alongside audio, not as a replacement of it.
ESP32 being visible means the MCU is powered on and booted; BT Audio being visible alone does NOT mean the MCU is alive, since BT Audio power is independent of the MCU.

Device presence is judged from 3 signals only: audio chip alone means USB cable is connected (audio is always-on, does not imply power-on); ESP32(wireless) or DFU device visible means powered on; only audio with no ESP32/DFU means connected-but-not-powered-on (updater reports "Cannot detect the device"); no audio at all means USB not connected.

ESP32 module VID is 0x303A (Espressif), verified on real hardware for both Reactor and Spark 2. Spark 2 shows VID_303A&PID_1001 (ESP32-S3 native USB) when powered on, and it disappears when powered off.
Spark 2/EDGE/LIVE have no CDC interface (ProductConfig cdcPid=""); the 0901 serial port they expose is a PG virtual COM, not CDC, and must not be used as a presence-detection signal.
Spark 2/EDGE/LIVE firmware updates go through DFU (STM32 bootloader -> DFU PID), not CDC, because these products have no CDC interface at all; only Reactor has CDC (0506/0507).
STM32_Programmer_CLI reports Device ID 0x485 / Revision B for Reactor's STM32H7RSxx (H7R3) MCU.
STM32H7R3 is flashed via SWD AP 1 (M7 core); ap=1 is the documented working configuration (per successful STM32_Programmer_CLI runs and flash-and-reset-stm32.ps1).
Reactor 50/100's external XSPI NOR flash base address is 0x90000000.

Passing --mass-erase together with -el <stldr> on Reactor's STM32_Programmer_CLI flash is dangerous: the -el/stldr path already auto-erases external NOR sectors, and adding mass-erase will incorrectly wipe the internal flash bootloader.
Reactor's STLink VCP debug serial runs at 921600 baud.
Observed bootloader prints itself as 'Reactor 50w Bootloader_version: 0.1.0.39' at this point in time.

Debug firmware always prints the label 'Reactor 100w fw_version' in its boot log, regardless of whether the actual hardware is R50 or R100 — the '100w' label is a generic/incorrect string, not hardware-specific.
Release build (RUNNING_MODE_APP) produces no UART output; the serial line going silent after 'Jump to APP code' is expected/normal, not a fault.

usb-relay.ps1 -Action pulse/reset drives the relay ON for PulseMs (default 200ms) then OFF; the relay rests OFF afterwards (code-verified usb-relay.ps1:45-49). Mechanism only: what ON/OFF does to the device depends on wiring, which is DISPUTED as of 2026-07-18 — memory 2026-04-29 modeled it as a power switch, but usb-relay.ps1 header (rev 2026-06-11) documents ST-Link NRST wiring (ON = MCU held in reset). Re-verify on hardware before relying on either.

On Alice's rig, Reactor CDC and ESP32 USB interfaces are powered through the USB Relay and disappear when the relay is OFF; STLink VCP and the USB Relay's own serial port connect directly to the PC and are not affected by the relay.

flash-and-reset-stm32.ps1 ends with 'usb-relay -Action reset -PulseMs 200' (a 200ms relay pulse). End-state power/run semantics depend on the disputed relay wiring (see RELAY:PULSE_BEHAVIOR). Do NOT apply the old "issue -Action on to restore" advice until the wiring is re-verified: under the NRST wiring documented in usb-relay.ps1 it would HOLD the MCU in reset.
The USB Relay serial adapter's COM port is NOT fixed. Discover it by probing (Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports, match the relay adapter's VID/PID). Never cache the number — it changes on replug.
probe: Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports, match the USB Relay adapter's VID/PID

fact: The ST-Link VCP debug port's COM number is NOT fixed. Discover it by probing (Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports, match the ST-Link VCP VID/PID). Never cache the number — it changes on replug.
probe: Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports, match STLink VCP VID/PID

fact: The ESP32 serial port's COM number is NOT fixed. Discover it by probing (VID match via serial.tools.list_ports or Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports). Never cache the number — it changes on replug; presence of the port also means the ESP32 is powered.
probe: python -c "import serial.tools.list_ports; ..." matching ESP32 VID, or Get-PnpDevice -Class Ports

fact: After writing STM32 external NOR flash, a full power cycle (not just a soft reset) is required for the MCU to correctly load the new firmware.
On Reactor, the ESP32's EN (enable) pin is controlled by the STM32; the ESP32 does not power on / enumerate until the STM32 has booted.
STM32 firmware running in production/release mode (RUNNING_MODE_APP) outputs no UART log; only APP_DEBUG-mode builds output UART logs.
Installing an OTA-delivered production firmware binary switches the STM32 from APP_DEBUG mode to RUNNING_MODE_APP.
STM32H7R3 is part of the H7Rx family, a chip series introduced in 2024, distinct from the older H7Bx family.
STM32H750 belongs to the older H7Bx chip family.
STM32H7R3 differs materially from STM32H750 in memory map, clock tree, XIP-from-external-flash behavior, peripheral set, and cache behavior, despite superficial ~90% similarity.
The reactor-fw repo targets STM32H7R3, verified via the STM32H7R3I8TX_extmemloader linker script and an identical ExtMemLoader source tree vs reactor-50-100-fw.
The reactor-50-100-fw repo targets STM32H7R3 (same chip as reactor-fw), verified via matching linker script naming and identical ExtMemLoader source.
Reactor 50/100 uses STM32H7R3.
Spark MINI 2 (Mini2) uses STM32H750.
Spark 2 uses STM32H750.
Spark Pedal uses STM32H750.
esp_http_client_read() is a blocking loop that only returns once it has filled its 1536-byte buffer or the TLS_TIMEOUT (7000ms) elapses, at which point it returns whatever partial data was read.

scope: Reactor ESP32 firmware OTA test tooling (throttle_server.py)
fact: To force esp_http_client_read() into a partial (non-full-buffer) read against Reactor STM32 OTA, the throttle server must use chunk-size=127 (odd, divides neither 512 nor 1536) with burst-size=10 (1270 bytes/burst, under the 1536 buffer) and stall-sec=8 (>7s TLS_TIMEOUT); chunk-size=1536 does not work because 10x1536 exactly fills 10 buffers and produces a clean timeout/error instead of a partial read.

If the ESP32-side test/reference binary (esp32_fw_reactor100.bin) is missing or fails to download, the ESP32 OTA task hangs forever on vTaskDelay(portMAX_DELAY) and the STM32 OTA never starts — a valid binary must always be present for STM32 OTA to be reachable via ESP32 pass-through.
Firmware-update device detection anchors on each product's USB Audio PID (not its DFU PID) — the same pattern already used for Reactor's BT Audio PID anchor.
Entering DFU/firmware-update mode, the power (BATT) LED breathes white (set_led_breath(LED_COLOR_WHITE)) — it does not blink and is not solid.
Entering DFU/firmware-update mode, the WiFi LED breathes white and the BT LED blinks blue (not the power LED, unlike Spark).
Across Gen2 products the DFU-entry combo is power+pair, but the power control differs: Reactor's is a switch, while Spark 2/LIVE/EDGE's is a momentary power button.
It is assumed (not hardware-verified) that Spark's ESP32 module shares the same USB parent/hub as its USB Audio device, which is the premise the audio-anchored ESP32 detection design depends on.
The real esptool source lives at C:\Users\alice\.espressif\python_env\idf5.0_py3.11_env\Lib\site-packages\esptool\ — the esp-idf/components/esptool_py/esptool/esptool.py path is only a launcher stub.

scope: PR review tooling (GitLab/GitHub MRs, Positive Grid)
fact: The gemini code-review bot for PR/MR review was sunset on 2026-07-17; /gemini review no longer responds. PR review is now done by running a self-driven subagent holistic review instead.
New-generation Spark's wireless module is Airoha AB1585/1595, not ESP32; there is no separate spark-esp32 GitHub repo for it.
ReactorFwUpdater's shared Subprocess utility has a hardcoded 64KB output buffer and a 120s timeout, which is a common constraint on every shell-out call it makes (esptool, dfu-util, etc.).

Promoting a Reactor OTA staging release to production is done by rerunning Release_Reactor_FW_FOR_OTA with the same BUILD_NO/ESP32_BUILD_NO and flipping TO_PRODUCTION=true — it reuses the already-built artifact rather than rebuilding.
Spark Gen 1 firmware-update handshake uses MIDI SysEx.
Reactor firmware-update handshake uses CDC (virtual serial), not MIDI.
Reactor 50/100's chip combination is STM32 MCU + ESP32 (WiFi/BT).
Spark Gen 1 firmware update uses the WinUSB driver in ADFU mode.
Reactor firmware update uses the WinUSB driver in DFU mode.
Spark Gen 1's firmware updater is SparkFwUpgradeLauncher, built on JUCE 7.
Reactor's firmware updater is ReactorFwUpdater, built on JUCE 8.

scope: Spark Gen 1 + Reactor (shared Windows platform layer)
fact: Windows 25H2 platform-level issues (Co-installer deprecation, UAC, driver signing) affect both Spark Gen 1 and Reactor equally, since these are platform-layer problems, not product-specific ones.

Windows 25H2 changed MidiSrv behavior (added the UMP protocol), causing 5 independent deadlock paths in SparkFwUpgradeLauncher during Spark MINI firmware update on Windows; all 5 were fixed by dedicated commits (79392d03, e57b2b5b, c7eea3e, fd837a3, and 92e7b4c/598ae04/e9db692/04caf8f). macOS is unaffected because CoreMIDI has no MidiSrv/UMP gateway layer.

Spark MINI's g1x-bootloader had 4 USB MIDI descriptor non-compliances (wrong wTotalLength=65 instead of 37, no reply to UMP Discovery Request, no bulk-IN data, 3 undeclared endpoints) that Windows 25H2's stricter descriptor parsing turned into hangs/deadlocks; all 4 were fixed (commits 5438843, 1a7b3c9, 9c5bd5d, ffe0819). Older Windows and macOS tolerated the non-compliant descriptor.

In Spark MINI's JUMPTOBOOTLOADER handler, I2S DMA (DMA1_Stream3/SPI2/SPI3) was not stopped before jumping to bootloader; on the STM32 build the static DMA buffer i2s2_adc_buf2 at 0x200057C4 sits only 20 bytes from the bootloader's MSP at 0x200057D8, so DMA writes corrupt the bootloader stack and HardFault. This bug is platform-agnostic (not Windows-25H2-specific); fixed in bootloader SystemInit() by resetting DMA/I2S (commit 4d42bfd).

The GD32 bootloader build has MSP=0x200057D8 while the STM32 bootloader build has MSP=0x20004720, even though the two branches' source is identical except 2 lines of chip selection in the .ewp project file. The root cause of the MSP difference itself is unverified — it needs a clean-build .map file comparison.
